Interestingly, we did observe subtle differences between exercise modes in the magnitude of stimulation, with acute RE showing higher phosphorylation of m TOR at 4 h, S6K1 at 1 h, and 4E- B P1 during the post-exercise period. It is interesting to speculate that perhaps these differences in phosphorylation status could be related to fiber type recruitment rather than exercise mode. Specifically, we ( ) and others ( ) have identified preferential mTOR activation in type II fibers following RE. Similarly, cycle training has been shown to increase basal mTOR phosphorylation in both type I and type II fibers, but the response is more pronounced in type II fibers ( ). Consequently, the subtle differences in mTOR signaling between exercise modes may be related to increased forced requirements during each contraction and greater recruitment of type II fibers during RE. To our knowledge, this is the first study to employ a cross-over design in untrained, exercise naïve individuals to investigate the early signaling response to divergent exercise modes. While there were subtle differences as discussed above, both acute RE and acute AE increased mTOR and S6K1 phosphorylation 4 h after exercise in these participants. These signaling findings at 4 h post exercise were accompanied by IPA analyses of differentially expressed genes indicating mTOR to be active 4 h following each exercise mode. Together, these findings indicate that in untrained individuals both exercise modes stimulate mTOR signaling activity in the early hours after exercise, which may represent a “general” response to what could be considered “unfamiliar” stimuli experience by untrained skeletal muscle (which may also explain the relative variability in some signaling responses). Moreover, we specifically focused our work on exercise naïve individuals as exercise training has been shown to alter ( ) or blunt ( , ; ) the acute molecular response of skeletal muscle to exercise, which may impede identification of early adaptive exercise-mode specific molecular responses. Consequently, to what extent similar findings would be observed in response to training requires further investigation. Interestingly, however, our observation of mTOR signaling being stimulated by both exercise modes in untrained individuals is in agreement with previous work consisting of single-leg exercise combined with nutrient ingestion ( ) and parallel-group designs in highly active individuals ( ). Moreover, the latter study ( ) only included a 1-h post-exercise time course, and therefore the findings from the current study indicate similarities in mTOR signaling following RE and AE can be observed for up to 4 h post exercise. Collectively, our findings in untrained individuals, coupled with previous work in highly active participants ( ), do not provide strong support that large differences in mTOR signaling play a primary role in mediating exercise-mode specific responses between AE and RE. However, mTOR stimulation can lead to a variety of potential cellular responses ( ), and thus further research is necessary to uncover to what extent the “downstream” response to mTOR signaling differs following divergent exercise. In addition to our investigation of mTOR signaling, we also examined the response of select autophagy markers in skeletal muscle. Our interest in specifically examining markers of autophagy in response to each exercise mode is based on (1) previous work demonstrating AE to elicit a greater overall proteolytic transcriptional response compared to RE ( ), (2) our previous transcriptome analyses indicating AE induced a specific gene cluster with several genes in the FOXO pathway ( ), and (3) suggestions that autophagy may be an important process mediating endurance exercise performance ( ) and mitochondrial biogenesis, the latter of which may be regulated through PGC-1α ( ). In contrast to our hypothesis, both acute RE and AE resulted in similar responses in the select autophagy protein makers, despite a lack of change in PGC-1α protein content, during the immediate hours following acute exercise. The lack of change in LC3BI coupled with the reduction in LC3BII and p62 during the post-exercise time course is in agreement with previous exercise-based findings ( ; ). Moreover, a change in LC3BII alone can be difficult to interpret as this can occur due to less conversion of LC3BI to LC3BII or through degradation of LC3BII in the lysosome. However, the reduction in LC3BII was accompanied by a reduction in autophagosome membrane protein, p62. Given p62 is degraded in the lysosome during autophagy ( ; ), the reduction in both p62 and LC3BII would indicate that both exercise modes likely stimulated increased degradation of autophagosomes ( ). The finding that both exercise modes stimulated increased degradation of autophagosomes would also appear to be supported by the drop in FOXO3a protein content, and likely movement of FOXO3a from the cytosol (compartment analyzed) to the nucleus where it serves, in part, as a transcription factor for autophagy-related genes ( ). While IPA upstream regulator analysis only identified FOXO3 to be active following RE at 4 h post exercise, we did observe several autophagy-associated genes to be upregulated at 4 h post-exercise in response to both exercise modes, which we interpret to also indicate increased autophagy involvement ( ). Importantly, this upregulation of autophagy-associated genes at 4 h corresponded to the drop in cytosolic FOXO3a protein content. It is important to note, however, that greater stimulation of autophagy may occur when exercise is performed in the fasted state ( ). Therefore, we cannot confirm that the fasted state of the subjects in the current study did not confound exercise-mode specific responses in the selected autophagy markers. Collectively, in the absence of any feeding, our data suggest that lysosomal degradation of autophagosomes may be similarly stimulated in the early/untrained adaptive response of skeletal muscle to both AE and RE. We acknowledge that the findings from this investigation are confined to the specific exercises examined. While AE, in particular, is not confined to cycling exercise, we specifically chose leg extension RE and cycling AE as these exercises are known to stimulate the skeletal muscle that was biopsied (i.e., vastus lateralis). We also acknowledge that cycling exercise training may promote a muscle hypertrophy response ( ) that may make identifying differences between AE and RE in mTOR signaling difficult as compared to running. However, it should be noted that running may also stimulate growth, at least at the single muscle fiber level ( ; ). We also recognize the impact that nutrient ingestion can have on the signaling pathways and proteins examined. However, the goal of this study was to focus on the independent response of untrained skeletal muscle to each mode of exercise. Future studies are needed to incorporate the impact of nutrient ingestion on the divergent exercise response. Although consistent with group sizes from previous work investigating divergent exercise responses ( ), this study does include a relatively small sample size. However, the use of a cross-over design reduces the overall impact of the smaller sample size. Moreover, to further minimize this potential impact on our conclusions, we chose to also perform upstream regulator analyses for mTOR and FOXO3 utilizing unbiased skeletal muscle transcriptomics to supplement (and strengthen) the immunoblot findings. This study also included only a single basal biopsy. This design was chosen to minimize the number of biopsies, and thus support the cross-over design which also reduces the impact of the single basal biopsy. Lastly, we recognize that the acute adaptive response to exercise lasts beyond 4 h ( ; ). Consequently, a longer time course following exercise is certainly warranted and may elicit different findings. In summary, our findings indicate that in untrained individuals both acute AE and RE stimulate mTOR signaling and result in similar changes in select autophagy protein markers in skeletal muscle during the early post-exercise period. Moreover, these protein-based findings are supported by transcriptome analyses revealing “activation” of mTOR and increased expression of several autophagy-associated genes following each exercise bout. Our findings provide support that the early adaptive response of human skeletal muscle to divergent exercise modes is not mediated through large differences in mTOR signaling or autophagy flux. Thus, examination of additional molecular pathways, a more comprehensive time course (including chronic training), or perhaps additional transcriptional ( ) or proteomic events are warranted to uncover the precise mechanisms mediating the adaptive response of skeletal muscle to divergent exercise modes. Supplementary Material
journal: European journal of applied physiology

title: Increases in Motor Unit Action Potential Amplitudes are Related to Muscle Hypertrophy Following Eight weeks of High-Intensity Exercise Training in Females
abstract: We examined the motor unit action potential amplitude versus recruitment threshold relationship (MUAP -RT) as an indicator of MU-specific hypertrophy following high-intensity exercise training in females. Participants were assigned to either a high-intensity exercise (EX, n = 9) or control (CON, n = 18) condition and completed pre- (PRE) and post-testing (POST) during which maximal voluntary isometric leg extension strength (MVIT), vastus lateralis (VL) muscle cross sectional area (mCSA), whole leg skeletal muscle mass (SMM ), and high-density surface EMG (HD-sEMG) signals were recorded from the VL during an isometric ramp contraction at 70% MVIT. The HD-sEMG signals were decomposed and yielded a MUAP and an absolute (ABS; Nm) and normalized (NORM; %MVIC) RT for each MU. Individual MUAP -RT slopes and intercepts were calculated for each subject. Changes in the pooled MUAP -RT relationships for each group were also examined. Finally, relationships among individual changes in slopes of MUAP -RT and individual changes in mCSA and SMM were examined. Training elicited increases in MVIT (+18%), mCSA (+12%), and mean and pooled slopes of MUAP -RT . The individual changes in slopes of both the MUAP -RT relationships were moderately to strongly (r = 0.48-0.68) related to changes in mCSA and SMM . Eight-weeks of high-intensity exercise elicited increases in MUAP -RT slope in females. Further, the observed change in slope was related to both VL mCSA and SMM of the tested leg. However, changes in slope for the MUAP -RT relationship were more subdued when MUAP was expressed relative to the absolute versus relative RT.
full_text: INTRODUCTION While it had long been established that motor neurons vary widely in size, the functional significance of this size variation was poorly understood until Henneman described the inverse relationship between a motor neuron’s excitability and its soma size in 1957 , and subsequently that a motor unit’s (MU) contribution to graded activity is determined by its neuron size . Based on this fundamental work, it is understood that skeletal muscle force production is largely accomplished via the progressive, orderly recruitment of MUs with increasing neuron size in response to net increases in excitatory input to the MU pool, known as the size principle. Motor neurons with larger soma’s display greater axon diameters and innervate muscle fibers with larger diameters . Therefore, larger MUs are also characterized by greater action potential amplitudes (MUAP ) and higher conduction velocities (MUCV) than smaller MUs. In fact, Hakansson et al. reported that muscle fiber action potential amplitude was greater in larger than smaller circumference fibers. Further, Tanji and Kato and Goldberg and Derfler both reported positive relationships between a MU’s recruitment threshold (RT) and MUAP . Consequently, it has been proposed that the size principle can be assessed by examining the relationship between MUAP and RT . Skeletal muscle fibers are remarkably plastic and display marked increases in both single fiber cross-sectional area and whole-muscle size in response to chronic high-intensity training. It is widely thought that fibers innervated by larger, higher-threshold motor neurons display the greatest plasticity in response to high-intensity exercise training (i.e., resistance training). This idea appears to be based on the observation of greater relative increases in type II than type I fiber size following resistance training and the apparent alignment of functional characteristics (i.e., size, force capacity, fatigue resistance, etc.) among fiber type and MU properties, whereby smaller, low-threshold motor neurons are thought to innervate type I fibers and larger motor neurons are thought to innervate type II fibers . Furthermore, while myoblast cell lineage plays an important role in establishing muscle phenotype, evidence exists to support the contention that external stimuli such as neural impulse activity likely also strongly influence muscle phenotype . Indeed, Dubowitz demonstrated in a cross-innervation study that neural activity has a profound effect on the structure and metabolic activity of skeletal muscle. Consequently, as previously posited by Pope et al. , high-intensity exercise is likely to elicit greater adaptation in the fibers of high-threshold MUs as an artifact of the dramatically increased impulse activity in these fibers required of high effort contractions. Accordingly, recent evidence has suggested greater adaptation in the peripheral properties of higher-threshold MUs in response to high-intensity exercise interventions . Recent technological advancements now allow for the discrimination of MUAP waveforms and their firing trains using automated decomposition of high-density surface electromyographic (sEMG) signals recorded from multichannel sEMG electrode arrays. Importantly, Hu et al. illustrated that a MU’s AP , determined from sEMG signal decomposition, has a strong relationship with its RT in accordance with the size-principle . Based on these premises, Pope et al. examined whether MUAP could be used as a non-invasive indicator of MU-specific hypertrophy following resistance training. The authors reported that (1) resistance training elicited increases in MUAP of high threshold MUs, and (2) that training-induced changes in whole muscle cross-sectional area (mCSA) were significantly related (r = 0.91) to changes in slope of each individual’s relationship between MUAP and RT. Consequently, the authors concluded that the non-invasive assessment of MUAP using sEMG signal decomposition is a sensitive indicator of MU-specific hypertrophy. Sterczala et al. recently also reported training induced changes in the MUAP versus RT (MUAP -RT) relationship. In contrast to Pope et al. , however, the authors did not observe a relationship between the changes in slope and changes in mCSA. Thus, additional studies are needed to further examine the utility of this marker as an indicator of hypertrophy. To our knowledge, these studies have also not yet been replicated in females and there are several factors that were not addressed by either study that may ultimately confound the utility of the MUAP versus RT (MUAP -RT) relationship as an indicator of MU specific hypertrophy. First, there was no comparative control group utilized by Pope et al. and it is not clear how stable the MUAP -RT relationship is across time. Moreover, in a recent study utilizing longitudinal MU tracking, it was reported that 4-weeks of resistance training decreases RT by ~15% . Both Pope et al. and Sterczala et al. normalized RT to the MVICs at pre- and post-training when examining the MUAP -RT relationship, despite the fact that strength also increased significantly (+9.6% and +17.2%, respectively). Therefore, it is plausible that the reported increase in MUAP observed for MUs with recruitment thresholds >40% MVIC by Pope et al. and the increase in slope of the MUAP -RT relationship by Sterczala et al. may simply be an artifact of decreasing RTs, increasing MVIC strength, or a combination of both. Finally, at present, only 39% of all participants among the published literature are female, and it has been proposed that this percentage is lower in resistance training intervention studies . Therefore, the purpose of this study was to examine the utility of the MUAP versus RT relationship as an indicator of MU-specific hypertrophy following high-intensity exercise training in females. To address the limitations of the Pope et al. study, we utilized a comparative control group and examined MUAP relative to both normalized and absolute RT. METHODS Experimental Design These data were collected as part of a larger clinical trial. Participants were assigned to either a high-intensity exercise (EX, n = 9) or one of two non-exercise control conditions (CON, n = 18) and completed pre- and post-testing during which maximal voluntary isometric leg extension strength, vastus lateralis (VL) mCSA, whole leg skeletal muscle mass of the tested leg (SMM ), and high-density sEMG signals were recorded from the VL during an isometric ramp contraction at 70% of maximal isometric strength. The high-density sEMG signals were decomposed and used to determine the MUAP and RT of accurately discriminated MUs. Participants in the EX group performed two sessions of high-intensity aerobic exercise training and two sessions of resistance training each week during the intervention period, whereas participants assigned to the CON group were asked to maintain baseline physical activity levels. Participants Twenty-seven untrained, young females (mean ± SD, age = 20.8 ± 3 y, height = 166.0 ± 7 cm, weight = 71.6 ± 14 kg) volunteered, completed this study, and had useable motor unit data (criteria described below). Prior to data collection, all participants were informed of the experimental procedures, signed an informed consent form, and then completed a health history questionnaire. To be eligible, participants must have been non-exercising 18–29 year old females who answered no to all questions on the PAR-Q for people aged 15–69 y and signed an informed consent form. In addition, each participant must have agreed to comply with the protocol. The study’s procedures were approved by university’s Institutional Review Board in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki. Isometric Strength Testing Before and after the intervention period, participants completed isometric strength testing on a calibrated isokinetic dynamometer (Biodex System 4; Shirley, NY) with the hip and knee joints positioned at 90° angles as described in detail previously . Following a warmup , participants completed two maximal voluntary isometric contraction (MVIC) attempts, during which they were instructed to kick out “as hard as possible” for 5-s. Two minutes of rest was provided between attempts. If the participant’s second attempt was greater than the first, a third attempt was provided following an additional rest period. The maximal voluntary isometric torque (MVIT) achieved during a 1,000-ms epoch of the subjects MVIC attempt was recorded, used for analysis, and used to calculate the trajectory for the subsequent submaximal ramp contractions. Following the completion of MVIC testing, participants completed submaximal isometric ramp muscle actions while tracing trapezoidal shaped torque trajectories. The torque trajectory increased linearly at 10% of MVIC·s , plateaued at target torque and was held for 10-s, and then decreased linearly back to baseline at −10% of MVIC·s . Each participant completed two submaximal ramp muscle actions at 70% MVIC with two minutes of rest provided between each attempt. Motor Unit Recording, Processing, and Analysis During each submaximal isometric ramp muscle action, high-density surface electromyographic (HD-sEMG) signals were recorded from the VL using a specialized 5-pin sensor and a 16-channel Bagnioli data acquisition unit (Delsys Inc., Natick, MA, USA). Before placing the sensor, the skin was carefully prepared by shaving, abrading, and cleansing with alcohol. The 5-pin sensor was then taped to the skin over the vastus lateralis with hypoallergenic tape in accordance with the recommendations of Zaheer et al. . A reference electrode (Dermatrode, American Imex, Irvine, CA, USA) was also secured to the skin over the C7 spinous process. The four channels of raw HD-sEMG signal were stored on a personal computer and later decomposed offline using the Precision Decomposition III Algorithm described by De Luca et al. and improved and re-described by Nawab et al. . Only MU’s demonstrating at least 90% accuracy, as assessed by the decompose-synthesize-decompose-compare test, were retained for analyses. For every discriminated MU, the decomposition output provided unique action potential waveforms for each sEMG channel, which represented a weighted average MUAP shape and amplitude comparable with those derived from spike-triggered averaging . From each of these four waveforms, the peak-to-peak amplitude (MUAP ) was calculated, and the greatest MUAP from among the four channels was used to represent that MU. In addition, each MUs recruitment threshold (RT) was defined as the relative force (%MVIC) and absolute torque (Nm) at which the MU first discharged. Based partially on the criteria described by Colquhoun et al. , for a participant to be included in analyses, they must have had a contraction at both PRE and POST that yielded: (1) ≥6 accurately detected MUs, (2) an observed RT range ≥10% MVIC, and (3) a significant MUAP vs. RT relationship (p ≤ 0.05). Muscle Cross Sectional Area and Leg Muscle Mass Ultrasound-based acquisition and analysis of VL mCSA has been described elsewhere in great detail . In brief, following a five minute rest period, several panoramic ultrasound images of the VL were obtained using a clinical-grade, brightness mode (B-mode) ultrasound imaging device (General Electric LOGIQ S8, Wauwatosa, WI, USA) and a multi-frequency linear-array probe (Model ML6-15-D 4-15 MHz, 50-mm field of view) at 50% of the distance from the ASIS to the medial, superior border of the patella. The images with the highest visual contrast were used for subsequent analyses in Image-J Software (National Institutes of Health, Bethesda, MD, USA). A multifrequency, segmental medical bioimpedance analysis system (seca mBCA 515) was also used to assess skeletal muscle mass of the right leg (SMM ), which has been shown to be highly accurate . Exercise Training Participants enrolled in the exercise group completed eight weeks of supervised, progressive resistance and interval exercise training. Each participant completed two resistance sessions and two interval exercise sessions per week, and all exercise sessions were supervised by qualified personnel. During the resistance exercise sessions, participants completed nine different exercises, including the bench press, lat pulldown, lateral raise, seated row, leg press, leg extension, leg curl, biceps curl, and triceps extension. During weeks 1-4, the participants completed two sets of 15 repetitions for each exercise at ~60% of one-repetition maximum (1RM). During weeks 5-8 of training, the participants completed three sets of 12 repetitions at ~70% of 1RM. Throughout the resistance training program, the load was increased by 5-10% if the participant was able to perform the prescribed load for two repetitions more than planned during their last set on two consecutive training days. During each of the interval training sessions, participants completed a 5-min warmup consisting of low-intensity cycling before completing ten separate, 30-s intervals with a 90-s active recovery period between each interval. The intensity of the interval and active recovery periods were based on each individual’s rating of perceived exertion (RPE) , and were set at an RPE of 7 and 3 out of 10, respectively. Following completion of the intervals, participants completed a 5-min cool-down. Both the warm-up and cool-down were completed at an RPE of 3. All interval training was completed on an air bike with a belt-driven steel fan (Rogue Echo Bike, Rogue Fitness, Columbus, OH). Statistical Analyses Linear regression models were fit to the MUAP versus RT data, which provided slopes, y-intercepts, and correlation coefficients characterizing each participant’s MUAP versus RT relationship at pre- and post-intervention. These regression analyses were performed using both normalized (MUAP -RT ) and, to further explore whether the changes in the MUAP versus normalized RT relationships were caused by changes in normalized recruitment thresholds and/or strength, absolute RT (MUAP -RT ). Separate, two-way (Condition (EX vs. CON) × Time (PRE vs. POST) mixed-factorial repeated measures analyses of variance (ANOVAs) were then used to analyze changes in the slopes and y-intercepts of the MUAP -RT and MUAP -RT relationships, mCSA, SMM , and MVIT from pre- to post-intervention in the exercise versus control group. Post-hoc Sidak-corrected dependent samples t-tests were used to examine the pre- to post-intervention change within each group in the event of a significant interaction. In addition, Pearson’s product-moment correlation coefficients were calculated to examine the relationships among every participant’s change (i.e., Δ = POST–PRE) in MUAP -RT and MUAP -RT slope, VL mCSA, SMM , and MVIT. Because we hypothesized that changes in MUAP -RT slope would be positively related to changes in VL mCSA, SMM , and MVIT, one-tailed tests were used. Further, the magnitudes of the relationships were interpreted as small (r ≤ 0.10), moderate (0.10 > r < 0.50), or large (r ≥ 0.50) . Regression analyses were also performed on the pooled MU data in the exercise versus control groups at pre- versus post-intervention, similar to the methods used by Pope et al. (2016). Specifically, all MU data were pooled among subjects in each group and at pre- and post-intervention before the average MUAP and RT were determined in 10% normalized recruitment threshold bin widths (i.e., 0-10 %MVIC, 10-15 %MVIC, …, 60+ %MVIC) and in 13 (CON) or 14 (EX) Nm recruitment threshold bin widths, which represented 10% increments of the maximum observed RT in CON and EX, respectively. To examine the influence of the intervention on the pooled MUAP versus RT relationships, the slopes and y-intercepts of the regression lines were compared using analyses of covariance as described by Zar and used by Colquhoun et al. . All statistical analyses were performed using Graphpad Prism 8 (San Diego, CA, USA) and IBM SPSS Statistics (v. 25.0, Armonk, NY, USA). The a priori, type I error rate for all analyses was set at 5%. RESULTS displays the characteristics of the detected MUs in the both the EX and CON groups at PRE and POST. Mean Comparisons There were significant Condition × Time interactions observed for the slopes (F = 12.1, p = 0.002, ) and y-intercepts (F = 5.6, p = 0.03, ) of the MUAP -RT relationship, as well as mCSA (F = 33.3, p < 0.001, ), and MVIT (F = 10.1, p = 0.004, ). The MUAP -RT slope increased in EX (+1.46 ± 1.42 μV·MVIC% , p = 0.04) and decreased in CON (−1.09 ± 1.01 μV·MVIC% , p = 0.03). The post-hoc comparisons revealed no changes for the MUAP -RT intercept in the EX (−43.49 ± 59.11 μV, p = 0.18) or CON group (28.40 ± 41.79 μV, p = 0.22), whereas VL mCSA increased in EX (+2.33 ± 1.04 cm , p < 0.0001) and did not change in CON (+0.08 ± 0.73 cm , p = 0.96). Similarly, MVIT increased in EX (+20.42 ± 12.1 Nm, p < 0.001) and did not change in CON (+0.62 ± 8.55 Nm, p = 0.98). There was no interaction (p = 0.14), nor were there main effects (p ≥ 0.39) observed for SMM . While there were also significant Condition × Time interactions for the slope (F = 4.7, p = 0.04) and y-intercept (F = 5.4, p = 0.03) of the MUAP -RT relationship, the post-hoc comparisons revealed no significant changes for either in the EX (both p ≥ 0.18) or CON (both p ≥ 0.16) group. Pooled Analyses The slope of the pooled regression line for the MUAP -RT relationship increased from PRE to POST (1.58 vs. 3.36 μV·MVIC% ; F = 10.13, p = 0.0098) in the EX group ( ). There were no changes in either the slope (F = 3.16, p = 0.11) or the y-intercept (F = 0.79, p = 0.40) in the CON group ( ). However, neither the slope (0.73 vs. 1.42 μV·MVIC% ; F = 4.89, p = 0.058) or intercept (F = 2.12, p = 0.18) of the pooled regression line for the MUAP -RT relationship changed significantly from PRE to POST in the EX group ( ). There were also no changes in the slope (F = 0.001, p = 0.97) or y-intercept (F = 0.28, p = 0.61) in the CON group ( ). Relationships A significant, large correlation (r = 0.68, p = 0.023) was observed between Δslope of the MUAP -RT relationship and ΔmCSA in the EX group ( ), but not in the CON group ( ). Similarly, there was a large relationship (r = 0.65, p = 0.028) between Δslope of the MUAP -RT relationship and ΔmCSA in the EX ( ), but not CON group ( ). Finally, Δslope of the normalized and absolute MUAP -RT relationship were moderately and strongly (r = 0.48 and 0.56, respectively) related to ΔSMM in the EX group ( and , respectively), although they were not statistically significant (p = 0.098 and 0.06, respectively). There were no significant relationships between Δslope and ΔSMM in CON ( and ). DISCUSSION This investigation was the first to examine the utility of the MUAP -RT relationship as an indicator of MU-specific hypertrophy following high-intensity exercise training in females. Our study also expanded on and addressed several limitations in the original study that proposed the MUAP -RT relationship as a non-invasive indicator of hypertrophy , including the inclusion of a comparative non-exercise control group and examination of MUAP relative to both normalized and absolute RTs. We observed significant increases in mCSA, the slope of the MUAP -RT relationship, and MVIT in the EX, but not CON group. The changes in slope of both the normalized and absolute MUAP -RT relationships were in fact strongly related (r = 0.68 and 0.65, respectively) to the change in mCSA, and moderately to strongly related (r = 0.48 and 0.56, respectively) to the change in SMM in the EX group. Further, when we examined changes in the pooled regression lines characterizing the normalized and absolute MUAP -RT relationships, we observed a significant increase in slopes in the EX, but not CON group. Therefore, overall, our data demonstrate that 8 weeks of high-intensity exercise training elicits an increase in the rate of change in MUAP with increments in RT (i.e., muscle force) in females. Furthermore, the magnitude of this change was strongly related to the magnitude of change in whole muscle CSA and moderately to strongly related to the magnitude of change in skeletal muscle mass of the tested leg. However, our data also reveal some important considerations for investigators interested in utilizing the MUAP -RT relationship as a longitudinal assessment of MU-specific hypertrophy, which we discuss below. The high-intensity exercise training program elicited significant improvements in both knee extensor strength and VL mCSA in the EX, but not CON group ( & ). The observed 11.8% increase in mCSA and 17.5% increase in MVIT in the EX group after 8-weeks are in agreement with the significant 7-10% increases in VL muscle size and 10-20% increases in isometric knee extensor muscle strength reported previously following 8-10 week resistance training programs in untrained females. One significant implication of the increase in strength in the EX group is that the torque requirement of the 70% tracing increased (p = 0.003) by an average of 14.3 ± 10.1 Nm. As mentioned in the introduction, this increase could confound the interpretation of changes in the MUAP -RT relationship and, consequently, we also examined changes in the MUAP -RT relationship following training. In accordance with several previous studies using the same technology , we observed strong, positive relationships between MUAP and RT for each subject at both PRE and POST (PRE, r = 0.70 ± 0.15; POST, r = 0.74 ± 0.13). Thus, in accordance with the size principle, as a MU’s RT increased, so too did its MUAP , and this relationship was observed both before and after the intervention period. Furthermore, both the mean changes and changes in the pooled MUAP -RT and MUAP -RT relationships support the conclusion that the 8-week high intensity exercise program resulted in preferential adaptation to high-threshold MUs. Specifically, while the slope of MUAP -RT relationship decreased for CON, high-intensity exercise training elicited an increase in slope for EX. Similarly, while post-hoc analyses did not reveal significant changes, there was an interaction present for the slope of the MUAP -RT relationship that was caused by an increase (+27%) in slope for EX, and a decrease (−18%) for CON. Furthermore, the slope of the pooled MUAP -RT increased in response to training in EX ( ). In contrast, there was no change in either the slope or y-intercept of the pooled MUAP -RT relationship for CON ( ). Thus, together, these data provide evidence that combined resistance and interval exercise training elicited adaptations in the peripheral properties of MUs which were greater for MUs with greater RTs, and this is likely because the high-intensity exercise employed herein predominantly stressed this population of MUs . Pope et al. previously reported a very strong relationship (r = 0.91) between the changes in slope of the MUAP -RT relationship and mCSA of the VL. In the present study, we also observed strong relationships between the change in slopes of both the normalized and absolute MUAP -RT relationships and the change in mCSA (r = 0.68 and 0.65, respectively). Moreover, we utilized segmental BIA analyses to examine the change in SMM , which was also moderately and strongly related to the change in slopes of both the normalized (r = 0.48) and absolute (r = 0.56) MUAP -RT relationships. Thus, while our data agree with those of Pope et al. in that the changes observed in the slope coefficients of the MUAP -RT relationships were related to the changes in VL mCSA, our correlations were not as strong and changes in slope were only able to explain 46% of the variance in the change in muscle size, at most. It is also worth noting that the changes in the MUAP -RT relationships were more subdued than those of the MUAP -RT relationship. This was evidenced by both the lower relative increases in the mean and pooled slopes in the EX group for the MUAP -RT than for the MUAP -RT relationship, as well as the fact that the changes in slope for the MUAP -RT relationship were not statistically significant. This may suggest that training-induced changes in the MUAP -RT are not entirely a result of MU-specific fiber hypertrophy, but could also be influenced by changes in normalized RTs and/or muscle strength. Indeed, Casolo et al. reported “substantial and consistent” decreases (−15%) of normalized RTs following resistance training, but did not observe significant changes in absolute RTs. Together, these results suggest that participants in the EX group were able to achieve the prescribed torques by recruiting MUs at lower intensities relative to their maximum torque capability (e.g., MVIT) following training, and likely explains the greater observed changes in the MUAP -RT versus MUAP -RT relationships. Overall, our data suggest that an 8-week high-intensity exercise program elicited increases in the slope of the MUAP -RT relationship in females. The observed change in slope of this relationship was significantly related to both VL mCSA and SMM . However, the observed changes in slope for the MUAP -RT relationship were more subdued when MUAP was expressed as a function of absolute (Nm) versus relative (%MVIC) RT, suggesting that changes in normalized RTs may play a role in the training-induced increase in MUAP -RT slope. Therefore, investigators should carefully consider the role that training-induced changes in normalized RTs may have when utilizing outcomes reliant on normalized RT (e.g,, MUAP -RT , MUCV-RT , etc) before attributing these changes solely to the dependent variable of these relationships (e.g., MUAP and MUCV).
journal: European journal of sport science

title: The Effects of Concurrent Training Order on Satellite Cell-Related Markers, Body Composition, Muscular and Cardiorespiratory Fitness in Older Men with Sarcopenia
abstract: Objectives Concurrent Training (CT) is described as a combination of resistance training (RT) and endurance training (ET) in a periodized program to maximize all aspects of physical performance. To date, effects of CT order on muscular and cardiorespiratory fitness adaptations are controversial. Owing to the age-related decrement in satellite cells (SC) which are critical for fiber repair, conservation, muscle hypertrophy as well as cardiorespiratory fitness, the present study examined the response of SC related markers to CT order in older sarcopenic men. Participants Thirty older men (age= 64.3 ± 3.5 years) were randomly assigned into one of 3 groups, ET followed by RT (E+R; n=10), RT followed by ET (R+E; n= 10) or a control (C; n=10). Intervention The training protocol consisted of 3 exercise sessions per week for 8 weeks. Blood samples were obtained at baseline and 48 hours after the final training session. Results Weight, skeletal muscle mass, lower and upper body power, maximal oxygen consumption (VO2max), Paired Box 7 (Pax7), and Myogenic factor 5 (Myf5) significantly increased, while were percent body fat significantly decreased following E+R and R+E compared to C. Importantly, the improvement in skeletal muscle mass, lower and upper body power, Myf5 and Pax7 in the E+R was significantly greater than the R+E group. Myogenin (Myog) and Paired Box 3 (Pax3) significantly increased (P < 0.01) in both training groups compared to no changes in C. Conclusion An 8-week CT intervention improves SC related markers, body composition and enhances power and VO2max in older sarcopenic participants, regardless of the order of RT and ET. However, performing ET before RT may be more effective at enhancing skeletal muscle mass, Myf5 and Pax7, in addition to both lower and upper body power. While both CT programs produced notable physiological and performance benefits, performing ET before RT during CT may provide the greatest therapeutic benefits for aging individuals.
full_text: Introduction Sarcopenia is a condition that is associated with aging and characterized by a degenerative loss of skeletal muscle mass, strength, power, and cardiorespiratory fitness ( , ). A decline in the number of both Type I and II muscle fibers accompanies sarcopenia, with preferential atrophy in Type II muscle fibers ( , ). Satellite cells (SC) are located inside skeletal muscle fibers and are critical for fiber repair, conservation and muscle hypertrophy ( ). Maintenance of the SC pool is essential in maintaining muscle health throughout adulthood ( ). Previous research has demonstrated a lower content of SC in older adults when compared to young individuals, as indicated by transcription factors (e.g. Pax3 and Pax7) and myogenic regulatory factors (MRFs; e.g. Myf5, MyoD, Myog, and MRF4) ( , , ). The age-related loss of SC content and function might have a significant role in the sarcopenic process since SC are responsible for inducing the fusion of myoblasts with myotubes or myoblast differentiation to form new myonuclei ( ). Resistance training (RT) has been reported as an efficacious interventional approach to contest age-related muscular decrements ( ). Indeed, numerous studies have reported the beneficial influence of RT training on SC content as well as SC related proteins ( , , ). However, RT has little or no effect on maximum rate of oxygen consumption (VO2max) ( ), an indicator of cardiorespiratory fitness strongly associated with cardiovascular risk ( ). Therefore, it is optimal for older individuals to perform RT with other training modalities to increase VO2max, including endurance training (ET) in order to improve both muscular health and cardiorespiratory fitness ( ). Since adaptations to RT versus ET are generally divergent and may be affected by various factors such as modality and participant's characteristics, it seems necessary for older individuals to concurrently perform both training modalities to maximize health. Indeed, incorporating RT and ET simultaneously in the same training period, known as concurrent training (CT), has been shown to enhance physiological capability ( ). However, although CT can produce beneficial adaptations, there are concerns about whether RT or ET adaptations could be compromised due to the presence of both exercise types within an overall training program. This concept termed the “interference effect phenomenon,” which has been supported by previous research. Specifically, several studies reported compromised muscular strength and hypertrophy adaptations to training in response to concurrently performing ET within an overall training program ( , ). However, other studies have indicated that these detrimental effects are modulated by the frequency and duration of the ET sessions and that interference can be appropriately managed with prudent program design ( ). Limited data have been presented regarding the influence of within-session exercise order on interference between acute RT and ET performance or chronic adaptations. However, a speculative rationale for either CT order can be provided when considering possible interference effects between the signaling pathways involved in adaptations to RT and ET (i.e., molecular interference) ( ). From a practical perspective, the fatigue and substrate depletion of ET could compromise subsequent RT performance, theoretically supporting a CT order of RT followed by ET. Conversely, based on the time course of metabolic signaling events, it could also be suggested that performing ET prior to RT within a given session may ultimately reduce detrimental interactions between divergent molecular pathways. Based on the transient activation of AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) after ET, relative to the longer-lasting mechanistic target of rapamycin (mTOR)-based responses after RT, performing RT after ET could potentially minimize the detriments of ET on RT signaling and responses ( ). In contrast to this molecular interference-based rationale, a prior investigation in elderly men has indicated that performing RT prior to ET may lead to enhanced skeletal muscle quality (i.e., force production per unit of muscle thickness) and lower-body strength gains ( ). While the definitive occurrence of molecular interference based on CT order is yet to be established, and the translation between acute findings and long-term adaptations is uncertain, the paucity of data indicate that additional investigation is warranted. Regarding exercise intervention studies investigating SC, it has been previously shown that 12 weeks of RT leads to an increase in type II muscle fiber SC content in elderly men ( ). In addition, combining RT in the upper limbs with ET in the lower limbs have shown an efficient strategy to enhance the SC content in upper and lower body muscles in elderly participants ( ). Furthermore, in a recent study, the authors reported that performing prolonged exercise training improves the acute type II muscle fiber SC response following a single bout of RT in older men ( ). Interestingly, it has also been demonstrated that low load RT in sarcopenic older men is associated with a higher muscle cross sectional area (CSA), increased SC content, and capillarization ( ). Combining high-intensity interval training after a single bout of RT does not interfere with the increase in type-I-specific total, and possibly active, SC content, compared to RT only in sedentary, overweight/obese, middle-aged individuals ( ). However, concurrent low volume RT (10 minutes) and high volume EE (90 minutes), compromised the SC response to acute RT ( ). To the best of our knowledge, no investigations have examined the effects of CT order on plasma SC related markers. In addition, prior studies have investigated the acute response of an exercise session on SC. Therefore, the present study was implemented to determine whether the order of RT and ET within a CT program would affect physiological adaptations to chronic training, particularly the response of SC related markers. We hypothesized that CT, regardless of RT and ET order, would increase SC related markers and improve physical performance in older men with sarcopenia. Materials and Methods Participants Thirty older men participated in the study. Inclusion criteria for subject acceptance into the study were: aged between 60 and 70 years, presence of sarcopenia (diagnosed based on handgrip strength values of less than 26–30 kg, walking speed of less than 0.8 m/s over 4 meters, and a skeletal muscle mass index of less than two standard deviations below the average young adult population) , sedentary (defined as less than 1 hour of exercise per week performed in the previous year) and free of major diseases (Parkinson's disease, cardiovascular disease, diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, etc.) ( ). Further criteria included being a non-smoker, not participating in hormonal or mental therapy, not engaged in regular moderate to heavy training within the past year, as well as not consuming alcohol, no dietary supplementation and non-usage of certain drugs (i.e. those influencing muscle metabolisms). Inclusion criteria were assessed by a physician using the Physical Activity Readiness-Questionnaire (PAR-Q) and a medical health/history questionnaire. The study protocol was approved by the Institutional Human Subject ethics committee and was performed in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki. Study Design A schematic of the study design is presented in Figure . Prior to baseline measurements, participants were fully familiarized with experimental procedures and were randomly assigned to into one of three groups: ET followed by RT (E+R; n=10), RT followed by ET (R+E; n= 10) or a non-exercise control group (C; n=10). Measurements were conducted at baseline and repeated following the 8-week intervention (48–72 hours after the last training session). All assessments were conducted at the same time of day, within ∼1 hour, and under the same environmental conditions (∼20°C and ∼55% humidity). Participants were instructed not to alter their regular lifestyle and dietary habits throughout the study. Schematic of the study design. Both baseline and post-testing were conducted between 8 and 8:30 AM after a 12-hour overnight fast and avoidance of exercise. 3-day food diaries were recorded before and at the end of the study in all groups Abbreviations: E+R, endurance training + resistance training; R+E, endurance training + resistance training; C, control; BW, body weight; H, height; SMM, skeletal muscle mass: PBF, percent body fat; UB, upper body; LB, lower body; Myf5, Myogenic factor 5; Myog, myogenin; Pax7, Paired Box 7; Pax3, Paired Box 3. Anthropometry and Body Composition Participants were asked to urinate (void) completely within 30 min prior to reporting to the lab. Upon entering the laboratory, body weight measurements to the nearest 0.1 kg using a calibrated digital scale were obtained (Lumbar, China). Height was measured to the nearest 0.1 cm using a stadiometer (Race industrialization, China). Body mass index (BMI), percent body fat, and skeletal muscle mass were evaluated using a multi-frequency bioelectrical impedance device (Inbody 720, South Korea). Participants were instructed to complete an overnight fast of 12 hours ( ), attempt to sleep for at least 8 hours and refrain from physical activity for 48 hours prior to the test. Performance Testing Strength testing took place 24 hours following body composition assessments and blood sampling. Prior to testing, research personnel explained the purpose of each test, associated risks, possible discomforts and the responsibilities of participants. All participants were instructed to refrain from caffeinated drinks for 12 hours, and food intake for 2 hours prior to the testing session; however, ad libitum water consumption was allowed. Following a light warm-up, using both general and specific exercises, the participants performed strength testing protocols for all the exercises used in the RT methodology (i.e. leg extension, leg curl, bench press, lat pulldown, lateral raise, abdominal machine crunch). The highest lifted weight and number of repetitions to fatigue were recorded for each subject. The number of repetitions to fatigue did not exceed ten. There was a 5 min rest period between attempts. The participants performed two attempts and their highest lifted weight and number of repetitions were recorded. The one-repetition maximum (1RM) was estimated using a prediction formula based on the number of repetitions performed to volitional fatigue using a given weight. 1RM= weight/ (1.0278–0.0278×reps) ( ). For power testing, the 30-second Wingate test on a cycle ergometer (Monark, 894E) was used to measure upper and lower body maximal power as previously described ( , ). For cardiorespiratory fitness assessments, the Modified Bruce protocol was used to determine VO2max as previously described ( ). Concurrent Training Participants in the E+R and R+E groups completed supervised training 3 times a week for 8 weeks, with sessions separated by at least 48 hours. The E+R group performed ET first, followed by RT; while the R+E group performed RT first, and followed by ET. Preparatory Phase Before the beginning of the intervention, participants in the E+R and R+E groups performed 1 week of concurrent training, consisting of three exercise sessions, for familiarization purposes. This phase allowed for the instruction of correct lifting technique and familiarization with all exercises and equipment ( ). RT program Following the preparatory phase, RT began with 2 sets of 16 to 18 repetitions at 40% of 1RM in the first week and progressed to 3 sets of 8 to 10 repetitions at 75% 1RM in the eighth week. Two-minute rest periods were employed throughout the study. The specific exercises included in the program were leg extension, leg curl, bench press, lat pulldown, lateral raise, and abdominal crunch (Table ). The periodized protocols were adapted from a previous study that investigated older sarcopenic individuals ( , ) and followed recommendations of the National Strength and Conditioning Association ( ). All exercises were performed on variable resistance machines (Technogym equipment, Italy). Resistance and endurance exercise protocol Week Weekly training sessions Resistance exercise Endurance exercise Sets Rest interval (min) Repetitions Intensity (%1RM) Volume (minutes) Intensity (%HRmax) RPE (Borg scale) 1 3 2 2 14–16 40 15 55 11 2 3 2 2 14–16 45 15 55 11 3 3 2 2 12–14 50 20 60 13 4 3 2 2 12–14 55 20 60 13 5 3 3 3 10–12 60 25 65 15 6 3 3 3 10–12 65 25 65 15 7 3 3 3 8–10 70 30 70 17 8 3 3 3 8–10 75 30 70 17 ET program The ET program consisted of cycling on a fixed-speed cycle ergometer. Sessions began at an intensity of 55% of maximum heart rate for 15 minutes in the first week and progressed to 70% of maximum heart rate for 30 minutes in the last week (Table ). A heart rate monitor (Polar S810, Polar Electro, Kempele, Finland) was used to control and measure the exercise intensities. The periodized ET protocol was adapted from previous studies on older sarcopenic individuals ( , ) and followed recommendations by the National Strength and Conditioning Association ( ). Blood Sampling and Analysis Prior to each blood draw, participants were instructed to complete an overnight fast for 12 hours and refrain from physical activity for the previous 36 hours. Blood samples (5 mL) were obtained from the cubital vein using standard procedures 48 hours prior to the baseline training session. Blood samples were collected into Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) anticoagulant tubes and immediately centrifuged (3000 rpm for 15 minutes). The plasma was used to measure the concentration of the SC related markers Myogenic factor 5 (Myf5), myogenin (Myog), Paired Box 7 (Pax7), and Paired Box 3 (Pax3). The following values were obtained for accuracy and reliability: Plasma Myf5 (sensitivity of 0.02 ng / ml, intraassay CV <8% and interassay CV <10%), Myog (sensitivity < 0.375 ng / ml, intraassay CV < 8% and interassay CV <10%), Pax7 (sensitivity of < 5.8 pg / ml, intraassay CV < 8%, and interassay CV < 10%), and Pax3 (sensitivity of 0.1 ng / ml, intraassay CV < 15%, and interassay CV < 15%). All concentrations were measured by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (MyBioSource, USA). Nutrient intake and dietary analysis Because of the importance of nutritional timing during and around the exercise sessions, dietary intake in the hours near (before and after) the training sessions were controlled. The participants consumed a banana, providing ∼0.30–0.35 g of carbohydrate per kilogram of body weight, as a pre-exercise snack ∼1 hour before the training session. Additionally, dinner was consumed ∼1.5–2 hours after each training session and was standardized to contain 1.7 g/kg of body weight of carbohydrate, 0.3 g/kg of body weight of protein, 0.4g/kg of body weight of fat ( ). These recommendations were based on the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics, Dietitians of Canada and the American College of Sports Medicine recommendations for macronutrient distribution (55–65% of total calories from carbohydrates, <35% of total calories from fats and 10–15% of total calories from protein). Protein timing was also considered and implemented during the study period, with ∼0.25 to 0.3 g of protein/kg of body weight consumed within 2 hours after each exercise session ( ). Beyond this peri-exercise nutrient intake standardization, the participants were instructed not to alter their dietary habits during the study. Participants were required to submit 3-day (2 weekdays and 1 weekend) food records at baseline and near the completion of the assigned intervention. Each food item was individually entered into the Diet Analysis Plus version 10 (Cengage, Boston, MA, USA) program and total energy consumption, as well as the amount of energy derived from proteins, fats, and carbohydrates, was assessed. Statistical analysis The normality of the data from the study was confirmed using the Shapiro-Wilk test. Based on earlier investigations, it was established that 10 participants per group would provide 80% power (2-sided α= 0.05) to detect 9%, 8% and 7% changes in Myog, Myf5, and Pax7, respectively [38–40]. Possible group differences at baseline were examined using a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). A time (baseline vs. 8 weeks) × group (E+R vs. R+E vs. C) factorial ANOVA with repeated measures was performed to determine differences between the treatments. This was followed by the appropriate Scheffeâ€™s post hoc test when a significant treatment and treatment-by-time interaction was revealed. Statistical significance was set at P < 0.05. IBM SPSS (version 24, IBM) was used to analyze the data. Results Participants Participants in the E+R and R+E groups completed 100% of exercise sessions. There were no significant differences in participant characteristics at baseline (Table ). There were no significant differences in total energy consumption, and the amount of energy derived from proteins, fats, and carbohydrates at baseline (Table ). There were no significant changes after 8 weeks for dietary intake. Participant's characteristics of at baseline and after 8 weeks Variables R+E E+R C Baseline End Baseline End Baseline End Age (years) 63.8 ± 3.6 ----- 64.1 ± 3.3 ----- 65 ± 3.9 ----- Height (cm) 166 ± 3.8 ----- 165.2 ± 4.2 ----- 163.7 ± 3.4 ----- Weight (kg) 62.8 ± 2.3 63.8 ± 2.3 61.1 ± 3.1 63.5 ± 2.6 61.4 ± 4.6 61.2 ± 4.7 BMI (kg/m ) 22.8 ± 1.0 23.1 ± 0.9 22.4 ± 1.2 23.3 ± 1.1 22.9 ± 1.3 22.8 ± 1.3 Data are mean ± SD. Abbreviations: RT, resistance training; ET, endurance training, C; Control. BMI, body mass index. Energy and macronutrients at baseline after 8 weeks Group Baseline 8 weeks p-value Energy (kcal/d) R+E 1989.5 ± 160.5 1985.3 ± 176.3 0.8 E+R 1993.6 ± 244 1987.8 ± 162.9 0.8 C 1976.6 ± 434.7 1967.4 ± 254.1 0.7 Protein (g/d) R+E 51.7 ± 7.2 51.4 ± 7.4 0.9 E+R 52.3 ± 13.8 51.8 ± 6.3 0.8 C 53.1 ± 16.0 51.1 ± 8.9 0.5 Fat (g/d) R+E 50.2 ± 10.4 49.9 ± 8.5 0.9 E+R 51 ± 9.7 50 ± 5.0 0.7 C 49.8 ± 16.8 50 ± 6.5 0.9 CHO (g/d) R+E 332.6 ± 19.4 329.9 ± 30.7 0.8 E+R 331.3 ± 32.4 330 ± 31.0 0.9 C 328.7 ± 62.0 325.5 ± 45.2 0.7 Abbreviations: CHO, carbohydrate; R+E, group performing resistance exercise followed by endurance exercise; E+R, group performing endurance exercise followed by resistance exercise, C; non-exercise control group. Values are Mean ± SD. Body composition and fitness parameters Significant main effects of time were observed for weight (p ≤ 0.01, η2 = 0.77), percent body fat (Figure , p ≤ 0.05, η2 = 0.41), skeletal muscle mass (Figure , p ≤ 0.01, η2 = 0.0.21), upper body power (Figure , p ≤ 0.01, η2 = 0.63), lower body power (Figure , p ≤ 0.01, η2 = 0.75), and VO2max (Figure , p ≤ 0.01, η2 = 0.63). PBF, percent body fat (A); SMM, skeletal muscle mass (B); UB Power, upper body Power (C); LB Power, lower body power (D) and VO2max, maximum rate of oxygen consumption (E) at pre and post-intervention *p<0.05 different from baseline. # P< 0.05 between group differences There were significant group-by-time interactions (P < 0.01) for weight [E+R= 2.4 kg (95% confidence interval {CI}= 1.9 to 2.9, d= −0.83) and R+E= 1.1 kg (95% CI= 0.7 to 1.3, d= −0.43)], BMI [E+R= 0.8 kg/m2 (95% CI= 0.6 to 1, d= 0.78) and R+E= 0.3 kg/m2 (95% CI= 0.2 to 0.5, d= −0.32)], and skeletal muscle mass [E+R= 0.7 kg (95% CI= 0.1 to 0.8, d= −0.57) and R+E= 0.5 kg (95% CI= 0.2 to 0.6, d= −0.36)], which significantly increased (P < 0.01), as well as PBF [E+R= −1.5 % (95% CI= −1.7 to −1.1, d= 1.1) and R+E = −2 % (95% CI= −3.6 to −0.2, d= 1.28)], which significantly declined (P < 0.05), following E+R and R+E compared to C. Importantly, the enhancement in skeletal muscle mass in the E+R was significantly greater than the R+E group. Among the fitness parameters analyzed, significant increases (P < 0.01) were observed in VO2max after E+R and R+E in comparison to C [E+R= 1.7 ml.kg.min (95% CI= 1 to 2.4, d= −1.7) and R+E= 2 ml.kg.min (95% CI= 1.4 to 2.5, d= −2.4)]. There were significant group-by-time interactions for lower body power [E+R= 40.4 watts (95% CI= 29.4 to 51.8, d= −2.5) and R+E= 18.4 watts (95% CI= 12.3 to 24.6, d= −2.4)] and upper-body power [E+R= 42.4 watts (95% CI= 28.1 to 56.7, d= −2.1) and R+E= 13.5 watts (95% CI= 8.4 to 18.7, d= −1.8)], which significantly (P < 0.05) increased in the E+R when compared to R+E and control. The improvement in lower and upper-body power following R+E was significant (P < 0.05) when compared to C. SC related markers Significant main effects of time were observed for Myog (Figure , p ≤ 0.05, η2 = 0.88), Pax3 (Figure , p ≤ 0.05, η2 = 0.71), Pax7 (Figure , p ≤ 0.05, η2 = 0.81), and Myf5 (Figure , p ≤ 0.05, η2 = 0.54). Myog, myogenin (A); Pax3, Paired Box 3 (B) and Pax7, Paired Box 7 (C) and Myf5, Myogenic factor 5 (D) at pre and post-intervention *p<0.05 different from baseline. # P< 0.05 between group differences. There were significant group-by-time interactions (P < 0.01) for Myog [E+R = 0.8 ng.ml (95% CI= 0.7 to 0.9, d= −5.8) and R+E= 0.3 ng.ml (95% CI= 0.2 to 0.5, d= −2.5)] and Pax3 [E+R = 0.3 ng.ml (95% CI= 0.2 to 0.4, d= −3.5) and R+E= 0.1 ng.ml (95% CI= 0.05 to 0.2, d= −1.1)], which significantly increased (P < 0.01) in both training groups compared to no changes in C. Pax7 [E+R = 69.9 pg.ml (95% CI= 58.2 to 81.5, d= −4.2) and R+E= 18.5 pg.ml (95% CI, 10.8 to 26.3, d= −1.7)] also significantly (P < 0.01) increased in both training groups, but this rise was significantly greater (P < 0.01, group-by-time interactions) in the in the E+R when compared to R+E. Furthermore, a significant group-by-time interaction (P < 0.01) was present for Myf5 [E+R = 0.4 ng.ml (95% CI= 0.3 to 0.5, d= −4.7) and R+E= 0.2 ng.ml (95% CI= 0 to 0.5, d= −0.7)], with follow up analysis indicating an increase in the E+R group when compared to C, with no differences between R+E and any other group. However, a significant time-effect was detected for Myf5 in the R+E group. Discussion This study aimed to investigate the effects of CT order and responses of SC related markers, body composition, and fitness parameters in older men with sarcopenia. Our findings indicate that CT, regardless of ET and RT order, improves SC related markers, body composition, power and VO2max in older participants with sarcopenia; however, performing ET prior to RT may be more effective at improving Pax7, as well as lower and upper body power. SC investigations have increased recently to assess their apparent role in muscle growth ( ). Although there have been experiments conducted to determine the effect of RT and ET on SC, the present experiment is the first study to assess SC related marker responses to CT with different exercise orders. Myogenic regulatory factors (MRFs) are a group of helix-loop-helix transcription factors that lead to the commitment of SC to myogenic lineage ( ). Myog and Myf5 are two types of MRFs transcription factors known to regulate myoblast proliferation and regulate terminal differentiation respectively ( ). The expression of MRFs is regulated by transcription of Pax3 and Pax7. Pax3 and Pax7 plays a key role in maintaining the proliferation of progenitors and preventing early differentiation ( ). Based on our results, there are significant increases for all examined MRFs and Paxs following CT, independent of the training order. However, the magnitude of the increase in Pax7 was greater in the E+R group. A limited amount of studies has been conducted to determine MRFs and Pax complex responses to exercise in older adults. Verney et al. investigated the effects of 14 weeks of CT consisting of lower body ET and upper body RT, three days per week, on the SC pool of the vastus lateralis and deltoid muscles in active elderly men. They found that combining RT in the upper limbs with ET in the lower limbs is an efficient strategy to enhance the SC pool in elderly participants ( ). Kosek et al. evaluated the acute and chronic effects of RT, performed three days per week for 16 weeks, in older sarcopenic compared to young adults ( ). At the transcription level, young adults responded with greater increases in Myog and Myf5 both acutely and chronically. The acute response to Myog and Myf5 was not significant among older adults, but there were improvements in both these SC markers after 16 weeks ( ). Furthermore, Kim et al reported increases in Myog but not Myf5 mRNA levels 24 hours after acute RT in older individuals with sarcopenia ( ). It has been noted that the intensity of ET, rather than duration, plays a fundamental role in the expansion of the SC pool ( ). In this context, SC seems to play a role in the muscular response to ET, which leads to muscle stress without significant muscle hypertrophy ( ). Nederveen et al. examined the acute response of SC content following high-intensity or moderate-intensity interval training in older men, observing a significant increase in SC content, as demonstrated by elevated expression of Pax7 cells, which are associated exclusively with type I fibers, 24 and 48 hours following ET ( ). Furthermore, an important association between cardiorespiratory fitness and muscle SC expansion has been previously reported in healthy participants ( ). A positive relationship was found between the amount of Pax7 cells following exercise suggesting that improved VO2max following ET stimulates SC expansion ( ). Although the benefits of ET and RT alone on body composition are well documented ( ), research examining CT in older populations is limited. However, evidence from a meta-analysis suggests that CT is the most efficacious means to reduce percent body fat ( ). Based on our results, both CT interventions resulted in declines in percent body fat. Previous studies in elderly individuals have also reported that equivalent CT-mediated decreases in percent body fat are achieved irrespective of ET and RT order ( , , ). In contrast, Sheikholeslami-Vatani er al. found a significant decrease in percent body fat when ET is performed after RT following 8 weeks of CT in obese young men ( ). The discrepancy between these observations may be related to methodological differences between studies or variations in participant characteristics, such as body composition or age. There is a different response in older than young individuals, which may be related to age-related changes in SC activation due to differences in the muscle response to mechanical overload ( ). This may lead to variability in energy expenditure, both at rest and during exercise, as well as a reduction in percent body fat following exercise training between these cohorts ( ). However, other factors such as a decline in oxidative capacity and a blunted response to sympathetic activation may need consideration. Previous findings indicate that body composition alone does not fully account for the disparities in energy expenditure between young and older individuals ( ). In the present study, skeletal muscle mass improved in both training groups, but this rise was significantly greater in the E+R compared to R+E group, although the magnitude of the difference was relatively small (E+R: +0.7 kg; R+E: +0.5 kg). Enright et al. found an 8.8% greater increase in quadriceps muscle thickness by using ET before RT during 5 weeks of CT in young soccer players ( ). In contrast, other investigations have reported similar increases in skeletal muscle mass and thickness following CT, independent of the exercise order ( , ). It has been shown that multiple interrelated factors mediate the hypertrophic process and that mechanical tension, muscle damage, metabolic stress, recovery duration and nutrient availability all play a role in exercise-induced muscle growth ( ). It appears that the molecular signaling associated with muscle protein synthesis (MPS), specifically the interaction between RT-induced mechanistic target of rapamycin complex 1 (mTORC1) signaling and ET-induced AMPK signaling has important implications for muscular adaptations to exercise ( ). mTORC1 is known to play an important role in regulating skeletal muscle mass in response to a wide range of stimuli, including mechanical stress, nutrients, growth factors, and hormones, mainly by regulating translation initiation and MPS ( ). In addition, the AMPK signaling cascade has been considered to be a major pathway through which ET may interfere with RT-induced muscle hypertrophy and strength in the context of CT ( ). Temporally, the final bout of exercise may dictate the molecular responses of mTORC1 and AMPK signaling and associated crosstalk between them ( ). In a rodent study by Ogasawara et al., where ET was performed after RT, inactivation of mTORC1 activity via simultaneous activation of AMPK signaling occurred ( ). They reported that MPS and phosphorylation of Ribosomal protein S6 kinase beta-1 (p70S6K), a marker of mTORC1 activity, increased largely when ET was performed prior to RT. Furthermore, increases in the phosphorylation of AMPK were observed only when ET was performed after RT [60]. In humans, Apro et al. reported that elevated AMPK activity does not suppress mTOR mediated signaling when ET is performed prior to RT. They also suggested that, rather than inhibiting mTORC1 signaling, performing ET after RT may instead interfere with the hypertrophic response by increasing expression of key components involved in muscle breakdown ( ). In the current study, upper and lower body power increased in both CT groups. These improvements were greater in the E+R intervention. One possible reason for the difference in the power differences observed between the R+E and E+R may be the greater increase in skeletal muscle mass in the E+R group. Indeed, it has been previously suggested that changes in explosive strength and power mimic changes in skeletal muscle mass ( , ). However, due to the small magnitude of difference in skeletal muscle mass changes, other explanations, such as the influence of CT order on quality of RT performance and the influence of the aforementioned molecular signaling pathways on adaptations, warrant consideration. Our findings are inconsistent with previous research reporting that exercise order does not influence power improvement following 12 weeks CT in young participants ( ). An additional factor that could have influenced the differential adaptations in muscular power is muscular recovery and the associated delayed onset of muscle soreness (DOMS), which may influence power parameters. Previous research has demonstrated significant reductions in power parameters associated with DOMS because of increased intramuscular pressure of edema ( ). Notably, ET performed prior to RT has been shown to increase heart rate and blood perfusion which decreases DOMS while facilitating muscle recovery ( ). It may be possible that improved muscle perfusion prior to RT in the E+R group ameliorated the adverse effects of DOMS on subsequent exercise performance and resultant power development throughout the study period. Overall, our findings suggest that upper and lower body power improvements are greater when RT is performed following ET during CT in older sarcopenic participants. The magnitude of increase in VO2max (R+E = ∼9%; E+R = ∼9%) in the present study, without any between-group differences, is similar to previous studies that investigated the effects of CT on elderly cohorts ( , , ). Since VO2max declines by 15% per decade in older individuals ( ), the enhancement of 9% in VO2max in both CT groups suggests a reversal of VO2max loss that occurs after ∼6 years. Therefore, incorporating CT in older individuals with sarcopenia could not only serve as an alternative intervention for muscle loss but also for a reduction in cardiovascular risk factors associated with a low cardiorespiratory fitness ( ). The current study has some limitations. Although we did not measure gene expression using biopsy samples, the improvement in circulating SC related markers (expressed during quiescence and proliferation) proposes a potential improvement in SC count and activity following CT in older sarcopenic men. In addition, the elevations in circulating concentrations of signaling molecules increase the likelihood of a receptor interaction, and thus a biological effect within skeletal muscle ( , ). Further studies with larger populations investigating molecular aspects in conjunction with biopsy samples can provide information on the precise effect of CT on SC content in sarcopenic cohorts. Additionally, we were not able to assess muscle thickness or cross-sectional area, which would have complemented the total body composition changes reported here. Our participants were older individuals with sarcopenia, and hence we cannot generalize our results to other populations or genders. Conclusions An 8-week CT intervention increased circulating SC related markers, body composition, enhanced muscular power, and VO2max in older sarcopenic participants, regardless of the order of ET and RT. However, performing ET before RT may be more effective at enhancing Myf5 and Pax7, as well as improving both lower and upper body power. While both CT programs produced notable physiological and performance benefits, performing ET before RT during CT may provide the greatest physiological benefits for aging individuals.
journal: The Journal of Nutrition, Health & Aging

title: Twenty-Four Months' Resistance and Endurance Training Improves Muscle Size and Physical Functions but Not Muscle Quality in Older Adults Requiring Long-Term Care
abstract: Objectives To assess the effects of 24 months training on muscle quality, size, strength, and gait abilities in older adults who need long-term care. Design: Non-randomized controlled trial Setting: Kawai Rehabilitation Center and Kajinoki Medical Clinic. Participants Ten older participants who needed long-term care (age, 76.7 ± 5.6 years) were participated as training group (Tr-group) and 10 older men and women who did not require long-term care (age, 72.9 ± 6.6 years) comprised the control group (Cont-group). Intervention Tr-group performed resistive and endurance exercises once or twice a week for 24 months. Measurements Using ultrasound images, echo intensity (EI) and muscle thickness were measured in the rectus femoris and biceps femoris as an index of muscle quality and size. Physical performance was measured before and after the training; performance parameters included knee extension peak torque, 5-m normal and maximal walk test, sit-to-stand and timed up and go test. Results After the training, there was no change in EI, while BF thickness was increased (pre; 1.82 ± 0.29 cm, 24 months; 2.14 ± 0.23 cm, p < 0.05) in Tr-group. Walk-related performances were improved after the training in Tr-group (i.e. 5-m walk test and timed up and go test). The percent change of knee extension peak torque explained the percent change of EI in the rectus femoris (regression coefficient = 1.24, R = 0.91, adjusted R2 = 0.82, p < 0.001). Conclusions Twenty-four months' training induced muscle hypertrophy and improved physical functions. Increased muscle quality in the rectus femoris could be a key to improved knee extension peak torque, with the potential to eventually reduce the need for long-term care in older individuals.
full_text: Introduction Sarcopenia is defined as the loss of skeletal muscle with aging, and this condition dramatically decreases the functional abilities of the older ( ). As a consequence, it becomes difficult for many older individuals to live independently, necessitating long-term care as progression leads to a bedridden state and/or death. As sarcopenia advances, muscle quality worsens because of the increased fat and connective tissue content within the muscle ( , , , ). The infiltration of fat into muscle, intramuscular fat (IMF or IntraMAT), is negatively associated with locomotive functions and/or glucose metabolism ( , ). Thus, prevention of and recovery from physical and metabolic deterioration related to muscle quality are extremely meaningful for the promotion of health in the older individuals. In earlier studies, physical training is considered an ideal intervention to preserve muscle strength and gait ability that are commonly used to diagnose sarcopenia and frailty. Several studies support the fact that training interventions positively affect physical functional parameters in the older individuals ( , ). Importantly, Radaelli et al. ( ) showed that thigh muscle echo intensity (EI) determined by ultrasonography in the healthy older significantly decreased after 3 months of resistance training. Muscle EI primarily reflects the IMF content and extramyocellular lipid (EMCL) as determined by magnetic resonance techniques ( ). Considering this finding, resistance and/or endurance training for the older adults would be recommended to help with independent living by improving muscle metabolism. Older individuals with sarcopenia and/or frailty requiring long-term care may have difficulty performing high-intensity and incremental training because of their lower functional abilities. Several studies have shown the effects of long-term training (over 8 to 12 months), regardless of whether the intensity was low or moderate ( , ). Therefore, training continuation may be the key to achieving the effects of training interventions. An interesting observation in these studies was that EI was significantly decreased after 3 to 5 months of training ( , ). Furthermore, we found that thigh EI decreased after 6 months of training, and it returned to the initial level after 12 months of training ( ). It suggested that the physiological significance of this EI fluctuation was because energy resources increased following the increase in muscle metabolism. However, the adaptation of physical training on EI for more than 12 months in this population remains unclear. The purpose of this study was to assess the effects of long-term (24 months) training continuation on muscle quality, size, strength, and physical functions in older subjects who needed long-term care. We hypothesized that EI continuously increased, along with energy resources of stocked fat in muscle, accompanied by the improvement in muscle size and strength. Materials and Methods Study design and recruitment This study was performed at the Kawai Rehabilitation Center, which was established as part of the Kajinoki Medical Clinic in Gifu, Japan. Importantly, this is a follow-up study to research reported by Yoshiko et al ( ). Participants in the training group (Tr-group) had difficulties in performing ADL without long-term care and were certified as requiring long-term health care, which is managed by the Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare of Japan. As part of their rehabilitation, they visited the rehabilitation center once or twice a week and performed several physical training exercises as described below. Participants who visited the clinic for regular medical checkups comprised the control group (Cont-group). We confirmed that Cont-group did not habitually perform any exercise by screening. Participant characteristics Physical characteristics in the Tr- and Cont-groups are shown in Table . With respect to physiological parameters, whole muscle mass was measured using the InBody 270 body composition analyzer (InBody Japan Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) through bioelectrical impedance analysis. Skeletal muscle index (SMI) was calculated as follows: SMI (kg/m ) = muscle mass/height . Physical characteristics in the training group (Tr-group) and control group (Cont-group) Tr-group (n = 10) Cont-group (n =10) Age (years) 76.7 ± 5.6 72.9 ± 6.6 Gender (men : women) 4 : 6 5 : 5 Height (cm) 154.0 ± 7.0 157.0 ± 9.5 Weight (kg) 53.6 ± 8.5 59.8 ± 12.0 BMI (kg/m ) 22.6 ± 3.4 24.1 ± 3.0 SMI (kg/m ) 8.3 ± 1.0 9.2 ± 1.5 Values are shown as mean ± SD. BMI, body mass index; SMI, skeletal muscle index. Ten older men and women requiring long-term care comprised the Tr-group. They had level one to level four requirements for long-term care, which they received at facilities certified by the Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare Ministry of Japan. The levels of care are based on necessary support time, with level one requiring the least assistance and level seven requiring the most assistance. Support times for housework, medical care, and ADL of > 25 min to < 32 min per day are needed for level one, and support times of > 50 min to < 70 min are needed for level four. Ten older men and women who did not require long-term care comprised the Cont-group. This study was a nonrandomized-controlled trial. According to the position of the American College of Sports Medicine, physical exercise is effective for promoting health and is recommended for the older ( ). We recruited older who did not require long-term care as the control subjects in this study because they had a much lower risk of developing physical and metabolic dysfunction by the lack of exercise intervention during the observation period.
